posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I was asked to start this thread in the Côte Chalonnaise thread. Great idea, but I have not drunk any wines from the Mâconnais lately, so you guys will have to contribute the notes for now and I will add some when you “make” me buy some of these and try them based on your notes.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Since I am the one who hinted at this and Howard picked up the ball (thanks!), I’ll throw a few TNs in there:

After I decided not to buy any Chagnoleau this time around (purchased some Badenhorst single vineyard Chenins instead), I saw the Pouilly-Fuissé Pastoral 2017 on a restaurant’s wine list last night. I had to try it. It’s pretty much a beast. The nose is showing a beautiful array of ripe fruits (white peach and some exotic), chalk, some sweet florals, butter and green almonds. It’s a deep and complex nose. A little intoxicating. The mouthfeel is dense throughout (ripe fruits and spices) with a very long finish. There is enough minerality and structuring acidity to keep it all in check but it’s a balancing act. Right now this is a wine that screams for food and with the meal (cavatellis with mussels and a sweet chili sauce followed by veal with gnocchis, chanterelles and creamed corn), this was a hit. I’d love to revisit this with 5 more years under it’s belt. I probably should have pounced on that 6-pack offer…

2018 Domaine Frantz Chagnoleau Mâcon-Villages Clos Saint Pancras: Good tension, very mineral (slight reduction?) but with nice texture. White orchard fruits (mostly pear), very discreet oak. Enjoyable and fresh.

2019 Gérald Talmard Mâcon-Uchizy: Did not like. I found the nose too perfumed and the palate lacked complexity and depth. Not my cup of tea.

2017 Olivier Merlin Mâcon La Roche-Vineuse Blanc: My only TN on this is: “Good QPR. Buy again.”. Bad TN but I guess decent wine.

2012 Château de La Greffiere Mâcon La Roche-Vineuse Les Ronzettes: This can age really well. Too bad this was my last one. The notes from élevage that were quite prominent when it was younger toned down; you still get some of it on the nose but the palate has integrated. It now shows good balance between the tension and roundness. Green apple with a touch of minerality and just a tad of vanilla cream on the finish. I like it. (TN from 2019)

2014 Dominique Cornin Pouilly-Fuissé: Premox alert on those (2 out of 4). But when they are not poxed, they are good. The entry is all about acidity but then the flavours develop and it shows nice chewiness. Citrus, pear, spices and some white flowers. It finishes nicely on riper fruits and a touch of salinity. (TN from 2018)

2010 Jean Rijckaert Pouilly-Fuissé La Roche Vieilles Vignes: Complex nose: apple, wood spices, white lily and something almost honeyed to it (raisins?). The palate is quite full with enough acidity to keep it fresh and with good length. There is no denying the wood here but it still makes for a nice ensemble. (TN from 2016)

link_counts.title: Frantz Chagnoleau - to buy or not to buy?

posts.name: Dennis Atick
posts.cooked:
1998 Domaine Guillemot-Michel Mâcon-Villages Quintaine - excellent aged Chardonnay

2017 Domaine Robert-Denogent Saint Véran Les Pommards Vieilles Vignes - still wound up, good potential (buy it)

2018 Jean-Jacques Vincent Pouilly-Fuissé Cuvée Marie-Antoinette Vincent - meh. Spend a little more and get Ferret (see below)

2017 J.A. Ferret Pouilly-Fuissé - we have a winner -wonderful stuff- punches above its class and pay grade (buy buy buy)

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
2017 Pierre Vessigaud Pouilly-Fuissé Vers Pouilly

  • 2017 Pierre Vessigaud Pouilly-Fuissé Vers Pouilly - France, Burgundy, Mâconnais, Pouilly-Fuissé (8/13/2021)
    Tasty chardonnay. Green apples and pear. Medium body with decent texture and grip. Pretty much medium everything: nothing overdone or underdone. Charming overall.

posts.name: Ashley Bair
posts.cooked: 2019 Domaine Michel Cheveau Mâcon-Chaintré Le Clos was really nice a couple of months ago. Actually, it tasted best on the third day that it was open.

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
Does anyone here have any experience with Andre Bonhomme?

We have a very small stash of Bonhomme, but haven’t yet gotten up the courage to open any of them.

I’m wondering what they’ll taste like.

[My experience is that everyone now is doing some sort of manipulatory cheat-fu along the lines of stirring-of-the-lees, and that most Whites these days need a few years of cellar time for the cheat-fu to burn off, so that the Chardonnay can start expressing itself.]

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked:
2018 D-J Meix Cadot VV was delicious this Spring but still could use a lot more time to flesh out and show its stuff.


Oops, just re-read the thread title… Maconnais, not chalonnaise

posts.name: Sean Corbett
posts.cooked:
Lafon Vire-Clesse and Thibert Mâcon-Verzé / Vignes Blanches.

The tropical side of chard done right.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Does anyone here have any experience with Andre Bonhomme?

We have a very small stash of Bonhomme, but haven’t yet gotten up the courage to open any of them.

I’m wondering what they’ll taste like.

[My experience is that everyone now is doing some sort of manipulatory cheat-fu along the lines of stirring-of-the-lees, and that most Whites these days need a few years of cellar time for the cheat-fu to burn off, so that the Chardonnay can start expressing itself.]

Bonhomme is pretty typical Viré-Clessé: slightly musky aromas (there is a lot of so-called muscaté selections of Chardonnay, which are aromatic [think hints of Viognier], in this part of the Mâconnais—and elsewhere for that matter), and quite a glossy, textural, muscular profile. A few of the smaller bottlings see some oak, mostly old, but for the most part vinification is in tank here (as for the vast majority of the wines of the AOC).

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
Got my wife’s family to adopt Jadot Macon-Villages as the house white and Pouilly-Fuisse as the fancy pour. Easy drinking and offers a lot more than many domestic Chards they were drinking.

Personally have had a few J.A. Ferret 2014s lately, still painfully young with hints of starting to show more fruits than they were a year ago.

Love this thread for ideas.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked:
Had some terrific ones this summer, all based on Gilman recommendations which were spot on:

2018 Pascal Bonhomme Viré-Clessé Vieilles Vignes
2018 Jacques Saumaize St-Véran Poncetys
2018 Guerrin Sur la Roche Pouilly-Fuissé
2018 Jacques Saumaize Pouilly-Fuissé Les Courtelongs

Tried a bottle of each and loaded up afterwards on all but the Courtelongs which was just a touch too ripe. The Guerrin in particular was terrific.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: Drouhin: Macon Villages, St Veran, Pouilly-Fuisse in order of drinking pleasure for me. The St Veran is consistently good to very good and might be the QPR champ of the three.

posts.name: SeanSydney
posts.cooked: Guillemot-Michel and, for the first time, Jules Desjourneys Pouilly-Fuissé and Pouilly-Vinzelles Les Longeays. All fantastic.

posts.name: Danius Barzdukas
posts.cooked: Thanks for this thread. I had the 2015 Louis Jadot Bouzeron (Domaine Gagey) the other night that was outstanding, like a burst of energy and flavor in the mouth. I know there are a few other well known Bouzeron producers, such as Villains and Ramonet, but it was so good that I was curious if there were others that are good as well.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked: For delicious value, I’ve enjoyed both the Drouhin and Auvigue “Moulin du Pont” Saint-Vérans. Have had the 2014 of each within the last year or so, and really enjoyed them. Have bought some in subsequent vintages based on those showings. Both can be found sub-$20, IIRC.

posts.name: JT Ng

2017 Maison Valette Et Pourtant

All excellent, the last needed a few days in bottle to open up fully.

posts.name: Thomas Keim

I drink a lot of Macon - my two current go-to’s -

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Thanks for this thread. I had the 2015 Louis Jadot Bouzeron (Domaine Gagey) the other night that was outstanding, like a burst of energy and flavor in the mouth. I know there are a few other well known Bouzeron producers, such as Villains and Ramonet, but it was so good that I was curious if there were others that are good as well.

This should really be in the Côte Chalonnaise thread!

But, Les Champs de Thémis is making very good wines in Bouzeron (albeit with some “natural” tendencies). Jérôme Galeyrand and Julien Cruchandeau are two producers from elsewhere who make nice examples of Bouzeron.

posts.name: Bill Tex Landreth
posts.cooked: $8.71 a bottle and damn tasty

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
2018 Jean-Jacques Vincent Pouilly-Fuissé Cuvée Marie-Antoinette Vincent - meh. Spend a little more and get Ferret (see below)

IMO the wines under this label are pleasant enough, but nowhere near the interest of the other Chateau Fuisse / JJ Vincent Pouilly-Fuisse wines. Likewise, you could spend a little more and get one of those. I think they would have been better declassifying this label, to better set expectations. IIRC it’s a combination of bought in fruit, plus some of their own from the other plots.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
An excellent choice of region Dennis - and FWIW we’ve yet to encounter any poxed wines from any producer at any level in the Maconnais.

One producer I haven’t seen mentioned is Rene Michel (unless it’s the same producer as Andrew Bair mentioned above), operating out of Vire-Clesse. It’s been well over a decade since I drank one, but imagine a producer that makes Thevenet look safe and restrained, and that’s what I got from their wines. I have vivid recollection of a 15%alc bottle that pushed things just a little too far for my tastes, but others were less extreme and much more exciting and with some botrytis to varying degrees in those bottles we drank.

Checking back, it’s close to 2 decades since we had a bottle , so who knows if they are still like they were back then

The entry is all about acidity but then the flavours develop and it shows nice chewiness. Citrus, pear, spices and some white flowers. It finishes nicely on riper fruits and a touch of salinity. posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
with some botrytis to varying degrees

We always tend to assume that this is at play, and some parts of Viré-Clessé are indeed susceptible to botrytis (especially the hamlet of Quintaine), but I think a lot of those exotic aromas are actually coming from “muscaté” selections of Chardonnay. This isn’t much talked about but these selections are endemic in the Mâconnais (you will also find them in places in the Côte de Beaune [old vines around the village of Meursault for example], Chablis, and even Champagne [Laherte make a cuvée that contains old vine Chardonnay muscaté], as well as in California [so-called Rued Chardonnay, which was one of the Wente selections that had muscaté properties, as well as modern clones which you find especially in e.g. Monterrey). What they have in common is being aromatic, as well as tending to producer higher pH, higher alcohol wines. However there’s a big difference between old massale selections and modern clonal selections which tend to be less subtle and less complex.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
I think a lot of those exotic aromas are actually coming from “muscaté” selections of Chardonnay. This isn’t much talked about but these selections are endemic in the Mâconnais (you will also find them in places in the Côte de Beaune [old vines around the village of Meursault for example], Chablis, and even Champagne [Laherte make a cuvée that contains old vine Chardonnay muscaté], as well as in California [so-called Rued Chardonnay, which was one of the Wente selections that had muscaté properties, as well as modern clones which you find especially in e.g. Monterrey). What they have in common is being aromatic, as well as tending to producer higher pH, higher alcohol wines. However there’s a big difference between old massale selections and modern clonal selections which tend to be less subtle and less complex.

Terrific insights William, very interesting indeed.

Responding to an earlier question about Andre Bonhomme: great quality and very keenly priced too. Their single vineyard cuvées come from seriously old vines (110yrs + for Hauts de Menards!) and the go light on the oak as has been mentioned. Tasted through the range a couple of times at a salon in Lyon and enjoyed drinking their entry level cuvées: Vielles Vignes and Les Brenillions. Style is rich and textural but in balance and there is a transparency in how they express their different sites. I’m still sitting on the single vineyards: I was told to definitely give these a few years before opening and Williams 5-15 recon seems about right to me!

Other recent Mâconnais:
Jacques Saumaize Vielles Vignes de Crèches Saint-Veran 2019
Saumaize-Michelin Puilly Fuisse Clos sur la Roche 2018 - impressed how this stayed balanced and mineral given heat of the vintage…
Domaine de Poncetys Au Clos du château Saint-Veran 2018
All really enjoyable but the Maconnais that really blew me away was:
Nicolas Maillet Maçon-Verze Le Chemin Blanc 2017 - stunning wine, I loved this one!

Overall, i’m a Mâconnais fan and have migrated my chardonnay budget as prices for my favorites in the Cote de Beaune have just gotten silly…

posts.name: William Kelley

Oops. Sorry for lumping everything together. I honestly thought of it as all one great big thing down there. It just means I don’t drink enough from there to study the region enough I guess.
Thanks for the recommendations William.

One big difference is that, until 2020, the Mâconnais didn’t have any premiers crus and the Chalonnaise did. This is because the boundary between occupied and Vichy France separated the Mâconnais and Chalonnaise. Premiers crus were introduced in 1943 as a way to preclude compulsory purchasing of Burgundy’s best wines at low prices by the occupying Germans (the armistice agreement stated that grand and premier cru wines had to be bought at the market price, anything else were fair game for compulsory purchase if push came to shove). So there are lots in the Chalonnaise, and none in the Mâconnais.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
What do you all think of Pascal Bonhomme’s (Viré-Clessé) wines? It was my first try but I really enjoyed the 2018 VV, especially for the low $20s it runs. It also seemed to handle the ripeness of the vintage very well.

They’re good! Farming is conventional but diligent, and they age well. Pascal and his wife split from the Bonhomme dynasty domaine a while back to do their own thing, and have nicely situated holdings (both in the tenderloin of the slope to the north of the AOC, and high up on the hillside on land that was reclaimed from scrub using heavy machinery [as in Meursault and elsewhere] in the 1980s).

posts.name: Dav1d S@wyer
posts.cooked: I’m suprised no one has mentioned Leflaive yet. Does anyone have much experience with those Leflaive bottlings? It seems that Leflaive is really making a push into the region.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
I’ve had quite a few in the last 10 days or so:

2019 Barraud Pouily Fuisse En Buland: Seems to have a lot of potential, and this is really early to be trying some. Not as good now as the below.
2018 Chateau Fuissse Pouilly Fuisse Le Clos: Just fantastic value and drinking, even at this early stage. Lovely balance, no sign of excessive heat for me. Yes, fruit driven, but would beat up a whole lot of Meursault at twice the price.
2018 Chateau Fuissse Tete de Cuvee: See above. It’s hard to know which I would say is “better”. Both just lovely.
2018 Guffens-Heynen Pouilly Fuisse Les Trois C: Harder and needs more time than any of the above at this point. Maybe great potential, but hold off.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Had the 2019 Domaine Leflaive Mâcon-Verzé a couple of nights ago. Really good. A recent 2020 Les Héritiers du Comte Lafon Mâcon-Villages was also very tasty.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:

It’s a catchy but naive line that betrays considerable ignorance of the history of Burgundy and how it came to be what it is today.

posts.name: Howard Cooper

Howard - thanks for starting the thread. I started to get interest in Burgundy in the early 1980s when there was no need to get into Macconias deep ( repeat : deep )…unless…

Yes - I am enjoying reading these treads - but that will be all…

I also started buying Burgundy in the early 1980s (started drinking it earlier thanks to my father). I think I have enough Burgundy in my cellar to last for my lifetime, but still have some interests in the Cote d’Or. In particular, there are some producers I still want to be buying and have gotten very interested in CM reds. And, based on a few wines that I have tasted plus a lot of posts from William Kelley, I am interested in what is going on in the Mâconnais and Chalonnaise. Some Burgundy lovers have moved to domestic Pinot and Chardonnay as prices have gotten higher. I hope to still be buying Burgundy, but they may not all be from the Cote d’Or.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
Guillemot-Michel and, for the first time, Jules Desjourneys Pouilly-Fuissé and Pouilly-Vinzelles Les Longeays. All fantastic.

Desjourneys:
2016 Macon-Verze also terrific. Excellent complexity for Macon.

Have 2017 Les Longeays on order.

Also (albeit not Macon) , 2009 Moulin a Vent, good if perhaps not typical Beaujolais (to me at least). Black fruit, garrigue, sage, almost hint of sweetness.

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
Opened a 2018 Les Héritiers du Comte Lafon Mâcon-Uchizy “Les Maranches” tonight - a superb value!

Yup, producer over appellation, almost every time. Rich, dense but kept taut by juicy acid, lightly reduced on the nose, layered with lightly buttered apples, slightly salty chalky minerals, and a bit of golden raisin on the end. Could pass for 1er Cru Meursault from a good producer! 92

posts.name: Joe D
posts.cooked: After more than a decade I’ve rediscovered Guffens-Heynen. Amazing wines.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
A 2019 Vire-Clessé from Rijckaert-Rouve last night. I must admit I have a slight preference for their Jura chardonnay, but this is still a super wine for the money and feels like it should age well in the short to medium term. Add to that Jean Rijckaert was an absolute gentleman when we stayed at his chambres d’hote, then the respect is strengthened. I never spoke to Florent Rouve, but considering his colleague had just brought some wine over from their Jura operation, I’m guessing that was him I saw (back then it was only Jean’s name on the label).

I will be seeking out the Pouilly-Fuissé wines (a [personal favourite region), though the national chain with a shop local to me (Majestic) never seem to have it in stock.

Would very much like to hear your thoughts on this, with or without snark is good by me.

I have had good experiences from mid 1990s to mid 2000s vintages when wines were imported under good conditions. Like the Thevenet and Goyard (Roally pre Thevenet) wines sold in NYC. And Daniel Barraud. I think Ferret import conditions were a little dicey back in the day per the other current thread on the Winebid auction.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
I deleted a post that in retrospect was rushed and too snarky. If I find time, I might add some more constructive thoughts later about how my Macon wines have aged over a couple decades.

I imagine I’m covering much of the same ground when I note that I wish my 1995 through 1998s from Ramonet, Sauzet, Niellon, and many other well-regarded producers from the Côte de Beaune had aged as gracefully and well as my Roallys, Bongrans, and Guillemot-Michels from those vintages.

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked: The 1995 Bongran had Botrytis, which gave it a certain patina, but one of the best buys ever was 1996 in magnum. That was incredible and pure. Wish I had more.

posts.name: Dav1d S@wyer
posts.cooked:
Had the 2019 Domaine Leflaive Mâcon-Verzé a couple of nights ago. Really good. A recent 2020 Les Héritiers du Comte Lafon Mâcon-Villages was also very tasty.

This past weekend I also opened the 2019 Domaine Leflaive Macon-Verze, side by side with their 2019 Pouilly Fuisse. I was very impressed with the fruit concentration and seriousness of both. The Macon-Verze was more round and has a slightly more citrus fruit finish while the Pouilly Fuisse had more acid lift and general poise. Both should be excellent to drink over the next several years. It doesn’t appear that Leflaive neglects these wines much relative to their CdB holdings.

posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked:
Thread revival time!

Has anyone had the wines of Frantz Chagnoleau? Looking to put a mixed case together at Sec Wines and saw they have several of this producer’s 2022s/2023s. Jasper Morris seems to like them.
posts.display_username: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Maciej Kocan
posts.cooked: I had a Saint Veran La Roche 23 a few weeks ago. Everything you expect from a Maconnaise, rich in yellow and citrus fruit (maybe popcorn?) and white flowers, dense but with good acidity.

reply_to_user.name: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked:
I have not, but in the spirit of reviving this thread - here is a bottle of Jules Dejourneys Pouilly-Fuisse that I drank earlier this year.

image1920×2560 291 KB

Insane QPR at $70. Has that dense chalky dry extract structure that you get from Raveneau, but a little more reductive with some new oak on the nose and a long salty finish.

reply_to_user.name: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: This thread and WK’s articles in the Advocate have been very helpful for me, so thank you. Big, big +1 to Desjourneys, Thibert, Guillemot-Michel, etc. I was at one point wondering if more or less everything from those vineyards was great. Is it helpful to hear about the one that proved otherwise? 2022 Château de Beauregard (Joseph Burrier) Pouilly-Fuissé Vers Cras. For me, it drinks like the most generic bourgogne. YMMV.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Thoroughly enjoying a glass of Louis Latour Viré-Clessé 2021 as I write: fresh, crisp and lemony, perfect for a hot evening! Very good value at 20 euros.
